issues.sentence: I said we were going to be the kind of people that other people borrowed money from.
issues.issue: Awkward phrasing - 'that other people borrowed money from' creates unclear reference and ends with preposition awkwardly
issues.fix: I said we were going to be the kind of people who lend money to others.

issues.sentence: Three weeks before the IPO, I bought in at the initial stake price.
issues.issue: Unclear terminology - 'bought in at the initial stake price' is confusing. Should clarify what was purchased.
issues.fix: Three weeks before the IPO, I bought additional shares at the employee price.

issues.sentence: The amount I bought was not large by Silicon Valley standards but it was every dollar I had access to and several I didn't.
issues.issue: Run-on sentence that could be clearer with better punctuation
issues.fix: The amount wasn't large by Silicon Valley standards, but it was every dollar I had access to—and several I didn't.

issues.problem: Timeline contradiction: The narrator says he bought shares 'three weeks before the IPO' at 'initial stake price' but also mentions he was given shares when he joined 18 months ago. The IPO opening price is $54, but employees buying pre-IPO would get shares at a much lower price (typically $1-10). The math on the Post-it showing a six-figure number doesn't align with someone who could only invest ~$7,200 total.
issues.fix: Clarify the difference between the initial grant of shares (from 18 months ago) and the additional shares purchased pre-IPO. Make the math consistent - either reduce the total shown on the Post-it or increase the amount invested, and clarify the pre-IPO share price.

issues.problem: Inconsistent characterization: The narrator is presented as financially struggling (can't afford honeymoon, borrows money, pulls from retirement) but then calculates a six-figure windfall from a relatively small investment. This suggests either the share price or investment amount is incorrect.
issues.fix: Adjust either the investment amount to be larger (making the character less financially strapped) or reduce the calculated windfall to be more realistic for someone investing ~$7,200.

issues.problem: March 2000 timing issue: The post references the 'new economy' and tech valuations in March 2000, which was right at the peak of the dot-com bubble. The bubble crashed in March-April 2000, but the narrative structure suggests this is the beginning of a longer story about holding through a decline.
issues.fix: Either move the timeline to earlier in 1999 to allow for the rise and fall, or clarify that this is happening right at the peak moment of the bubble.

coherence.rewritten_body:
I did the math on a Post-it note in the kitchen the morning the stock opened at fifty-four.

I did it twice because I didn't believe it the first time. Then a third time because my hand was shaking and I'd written a six where a five should have been. The number at the bottom of the Post-it was bigger than any paycheck I'd ever earned. Not bigger than all of them combined—I wasn't that lucky—but bigger than any single one.

I stuck the Post-it to the fridge with a magnet shaped like a pineapple. The pineapple magnet had come from a hotel in Maui on a honeymoon we couldn't really afford. It had been holding up a coupon for Bertolli pasta sauce for about eight months. I moved the coupon. The pineapple was now load-bearing.

My wife came in wearing the blue robe with the hole in the elbow. She looked at the Post-it. She looked at me. She asked if we were going to be okay. I said we were going to be better than okay. I said we were going to be the kind of people that other people borrowed money from. She laughed the way you laugh at someone who's just told you they're going to be an astronaut.

I was thirty-one years old. I had been at the company for about eighteen months. It was a real company. We made software that did a specific, boring thing for hospitals. We had customers. We had contracts. We had a CFO who wore brown shoes with a black belt and never smiled.

The founder, a guy in his early forties who wore the same gray Patagonia vest every day like it was a priestly garment, had given me 500 shares when I joined. Not many by company standards, but enough to matter if things went well. He said he liked that I'd worked the weekend of the Charlotte launch. I'd worked the weekend of the Charlotte launch because my wife and I had been fighting and I didn't want to go home. He thought I had hustle. I had marital problems. The market doesn't care which one made you stay late.

Three weeks before the IPO, employees were allowed to buy additional shares at $2.50 each—the same price as the Series C round six months earlier. The amount I bought was not large by Silicon Valley standards but it was every dollar I had access to and several I didn't. I pulled $1,800 out of a Roth IRA and paid the penalty. I put $1,400 on a credit card with a 19.9% rate. And I borrowed four grand from my brother-in-law and told him it was for a transmission on my Civic. That bought me another 2,880 shares.

The Civic was fine. The transmission was fine. The Civic is now in a junkyard in Reno and the transmission outlived our marriage and the dog.

That morning, March of 1999, the pineapple magnet was holding up a Post-it that said, in my own handwriting: $182,520. My 500 original shares plus 2,880 purchased shares, times fifty-four dollars. I stood in the kitchen in my socks. The coffeemaker beeped. Somewhere on the radio a man was talking about how the new economy had rewritten the rules of valuation.

I believed him. I wanted to believe him. The pineapple believed him too.

I didn't sell a single share that day. I didn't need to. The lockup ran six months, and at the end of those six months the price would be even higher, and then I would sell, and then I would buy the house with the foyer, and then I would tell my brother-in-law the truth and we would laugh about the transmission.

None of those things happened in the order I planned.

issues.sentence: The company was fine. That was the worst part. The company was actually fine.
issues.issue: Repetitive sentence structure - three very short declarative sentences in a row creates a choppy rhythm
issues.fix: The company was fine—that was the worst part. We were actually doing well.

issues.sentence: Dan saw exactly what was happening. Dan saw a thirty-one-year-old with a pineapple magnet and a Post-it and a story he was telling himself about foyers.
issues.issue: Starting consecutive sentences with 'Dan saw' creates unnecessary repetition
issues.fix: Dan saw exactly what was happening: a thirty-one-year-old with a pineapple magnet and a Post-it, telling himself stories about foyers.

issues.sentence: I told myself forty-seven was fine. Forty-seven was a coiled spring. Forty-seven was a launching pad.
issues.issue: Repetitive sentence structure with three sentences beginning with variations of 'Forty-seven was'
issues.fix: I told myself forty-seven was fine—a coiled spring, a launching pad.

issues.problem: The narrator references 'a pineapple magnet and a Post-it and a story he was telling himself about foyers' without any prior context. These details appear nowhere else in the text and seem disconnected from the narrative.
issues.fix: Either add context earlier in the story to explain what these items represent, or replace with details that have been established in the narrative (like his obsessive stock checking behavior or the company's hospital deals).

coherence.rewritten_body:
By August the stock had been to fifty-eight and back to forty-one twice and I had started lying about how often I checked it.

I told my wife I checked it once a day. I checked it eleven times a day. I checked it in the bathroom on my Palm Pilot. I checked it in the car at red lights using a service called Quote.com that sent SMS replies if you texted a ticker. I checked it during my niece's baptism. I checked it during a dental cleaning. I checked it in bed at 2 a.m. and told my wife it was a work email when she rolled over.

The company was fine. That was the worst part. The company was actually fine. We were signing hospitals. The Charlotte deal had expanded. The CFO had stopped wearing brown shoes with a black belt, which I took as a sign of growing professionalism. Our quarterly numbers came in slightly ahead of guidance and the stock dropped six dollars on the day of the announcement because some analyst in Manhattan had wanted slightly more ahead of guidance than we'd delivered.

That was when I should have noticed. The disconnect. Real company, real customers, real revenue, and the stock moving like a coin flip in a wind tunnel. But I didn't think about it that way. I thought: it'll come back. The fundamentals are sound. I had learned the phrase "the fundamentals are sound" from a CNBC anchor and I deployed it on my wife, my brother-in-law, my dad, and a guy at a Labor Day cookout who hadn't asked.

Dan from finance pulled me aside at the kitchenette in early September.

Dan was maybe forty-five. He wore short-sleeved button-downs and had a daughter at Penn State. He had been at three companies before ours, all of which had IPO'd, two of which had cratered. He poured himself coffee out of the carafe that always had a film on top because nobody cleaned it. He looked at me the way a vet looks at a dog he's about to give bad news about.

He said, "Lou. The lockup lifts on the twenty-third."

I said I knew.

He said, "When it lifts, sell half. Don't think. Don't pray. Don't check the price that morning. Put in a limit order the night before for half your shares at market open and walk away from the terminal."

I said, "Half feels like a lot."

He said, "Half feels like a lot until you're sitting on zero."

Then he did this thing where he tapped the side of his coffee mug twice with his ring finger. I don't know why I remember that. I remember the tap. I remember the film on the coffee. I remember his short-sleeved shirt was light blue with a pen mark over the pocket. I remember thinking: Dan doesn't understand. Dan has been burned before. Dan is a finance guy, not a product guy. Dan doesn't see what's happening with hospitals in the southeast.

Dan saw exactly what was happening. Dan saw a thirty-one-year-old who checked stock prices eleven times a day and told himself stories about market fundamentals while ignoring obvious warning signs.

I nodded the way you nod when someone tells you to wear sunscreen. I made the face people make when they've already decided to ignore the advice but want the conversation to end. Dan saw that face too. He drank his coffee. He walked back to his desk.

I went to my desk and refreshed Yahoo Finance. The stock was at forty-seven. I told myself forty-seven was fine. Forty-seven was a coiled spring. Forty-seven was a launching pad. I had a list of words for forty-seven and not one of them was "warning."

I also did not put in the limit order that night. Or the night after. Or the night after that.

Next: lockup day. The mouse. The forty-five seconds.

issues.sentence: The stock had closed at $45.20 the day before.
issues.issue: Jarring transition - this important detail feels disconnected from the previous paragraph about his appearance
issues.fix: Add a transitional phrase or integrate this information more smoothly into the narrative flow

issues.sentence: I had seventy-six minutes to do nothing. I did not do nothing.
issues.issue: Awkward double negative construction that doesn't flow well when read aloud
issues.fix: I had seventy-six minutes to wait. I couldn't just wait.

issues.sentence: I went to the kitchenette and Dan was not there, which felt like a sign, although a sign of what I couldn't say.
issues.issue: Convoluted sentence structure with unclear meaning
issues.fix: I went to the kitchenette. Dan wasn't there. It felt like a sign, though I couldn't say of what.

issues.sentence: I had a thing I told myself called the eleven a.m. rule, which I had invented at approximately 9:32 a.m. that morning.
issues.issue: Awkward phrasing with 'I had a thing I told myself' - doesn't flow naturally
issues.fix: I invented something I called the eleven a.m. rule at approximately 9:32 a.m. that morning.

issues.sentence: A new floor had been established mentally, retroactively, by me, in my chair, eating Altoids.
issues.issue: Overly convoluted sentence with too many modifying phrases
issues.fix: Sitting in my chair eating Altoids, I mentally established a new floor, retroactively.

issues.sentence: I sat there for what I now know was forty-five seconds because the screensaver kicked in at sixty and I had to move the mouse to keep it from going dark.
issues.issue: Long, convoluted sentence that's hard to follow - the timing explanation is confusing
issues.fix: I sat there for forty-five seconds. I know this because I had to move the mouse before the screensaver kicked in.

readability.rewritten_body:
Lockup day was a Tuesday and I wore a tie to work for no reason.

I didn't usually wear a tie. Nobody at the company wore a tie except the CFO and a guy in sales we called Powerpoint Greg. But that morning I knotted a navy tie with little white anchors on it and stood in front of the mirror and told my reflection that today was a serious day requiring serious decisions. My reflection looked like a guy who'd lost six pounds in three weeks from refreshing Yahoo Finance.

The stock had closed at $45.20 the day before—the number I'd been watching for weeks.

Dan's plan, which I had spent six weeks talking myself out of, was to put in a limit order the night before for half my shares at market open and walk away. I had not done this. I had instead spent the previous evening reading a Motley Fool message board where a user named CoiledSpring2000 had laid out, with charts, why our stock was about to triple by Christmas.

I got to my desk at 8:14 a.m. Eastern. The market opened at 9:30. I had seventy-six minutes to wait. I couldn't just wait. I opened the trade window. I typed in the number of shares that represented half my position. I stared at the field. I closed the window. I opened it again. I typed in a smaller number. I stared at it. I closed the window. I went to the kitchenette. Dan wasn't there. It felt like a sign, though I couldn't say of what.

9:30 hit. The stock opened at $44.10. Down a dollar from the close. I told myself that was fine. Mornings were jittery. Wait for the afternoon. I invented something I called the eleven a.m. rule at approximately 9:32 a.m. that morning.

Eleven a.m. came. The stock was at $42.80. I told myself I'd sell at forty-five. Forty-five had been the close yesterday. Forty-five was a fair number. Forty-five was the floor.

It did not hit forty-five.

It hit 43.10 at noon and I went to lunch with a guy from product and ate a turkey sandwich I do not remember tasting. When I came back it was at 41.90. I told myself I'd sell at forty-three. Forty-three was a fair number. Forty-three was a respectable number. Sitting in my chair eating Altoids, I mentally established a new floor, retroactively.

It did not hit forty-three.

By 3:15 it was at 40.50 and I had moved my mental floor to forty-one and was sweating through the navy tie. I opened the trade window one more time. I typed in the shares. My finger was on the mouse. The screen showed the order ready to go. All I had to do was click.

I did not click.

I sat there for forty-five seconds. I know this because I had to move the mouse before the screensaver kicked in. I moved the mouse. The trade window was still there. The shares were still typed in. The price had ticked down to 40.30. I told myself I would not sell at a price lower than forty-five. I told myself selling at forty was capitulation. I told myself I had not come this far to lose three dollars a share.

I closed the trade window.

The stock closed that day at 39.80. I went home and didn't tell my wife I hadn't sold. I told her the market was choppy and I was going to wait for a better print. She looked at me the way she'd looked at me the morning of the Post-it, but the laugh was gone.

Next month it was at twenty-eight. Next quarter, fifteen. I held. I held the way a man holds a steering wheel as the car goes over the cliff.

issues.problem: The narrator mentions 'the morning of the Post-it' as a reference point his wife would understand, but this event has never been introduced or explained in the narrative
issues.fix: Either remove this reference entirely, or briefly establish what 'the Post-it' incident was earlier in the story

issues.problem: The narrator says he 'had not come this far to lose three dollars a share' when the stock was at $40.30, but this reasoning doesn't align with his original purchase price or current loss situation - he's already lost over $4 per share from the previous day's close
issues.fix: Clarify what 'three dollars a share' refers to, or change the reasoning to better reflect his actual loss situation

coherence.rewritten_body:
Lockup day was a Tuesday and I wore a tie to work for no reason.

I didn't usually wear a tie. Nobody at the company wore a tie except the CFO and a guy in sales we called Powerpoint Greg. But that morning I knotted a navy tie with little white anchors on it and stood in front of the mirror and told my reflection that today was a serious day requiring serious decisions. My reflection looked like a guy who'd lost six pounds in three weeks from refreshing Yahoo Finance.

The stock had closed at $45.20 the day before.

Dan's plan, which I had spent six weeks talking myself out of, was to put in a limit order the night before for half my shares at market open and walk away. I had not done this. I had instead spent the previous evening reading a Motley Fool message board where a user named CoiledSpring2000 had laid out, with charts, why our stock was about to triple by Christmas.

I got to my desk at 8:14 a.m. Eastern. The market opened at 9:30. I had seventy-six minutes to do nothing. I did not do nothing. I opened the trade window. I typed in the number of shares that represented half my position. I stared at the field. I closed the window. I opened it again. I typed in a smaller number. I stared at it. I closed the window. I went to the kitchenette and Dan was not there, which felt like a sign, although a sign of what I couldn't say.

9:30 hit. The stock opened at $44.10. Down a dollar from the close. I told myself that was fine. Mornings were jittery. Wait for the afternoon. I had a thing I told myself called the eleven a.m. rule, which I had invented at approximately 9:32 a.m. that morning.

Eleven a.m. came. The stock was at $42.80. I told myself I'd sell at forty-five. Forty-five had been the close yesterday. Forty-five was a fair number. Forty-five was the floor.

It did not hit forty-five.

It hit 43.10 at noon and I went to lunch with a guy from product and ate a turkey sandwich I do not remember tasting. When I came back it was at 41.90. I told myself I'd sell at forty-three. Forty-three was a fair number. Forty-three was a respectable number. A new floor had been established mentally, retroactively, by me, in my chair, eating Altoids.

It did not hit forty-three.

By 3:15 it was at 40.50 and I had moved my mental floor to forty-one and was sweating through the navy tie. I opened the trade window one more time. I typed in the shares. My finger was on the mouse. The screen showed the order ready to go. All I had to do was click.

I did not click.

I sat there for what I now know was forty-five seconds because the screensaver kicked in at sixty and I had to move the mouse to keep it from going dark. I moved the mouse. The trade window was still there. The shares were still typed in. The price had ticked down to 40.30. I told myself I would not sell at a price that felt like surrender. I told myself selling now was capitulation. I told myself I had not come this far to walk away at a loss.

I closed the trade window.

The stock closed that day at 39.80. I went home and didn't tell my wife I hadn't sold. I told her the market was choppy and I was going to wait for a better print. She looked at me with a mixture of concern and disappointment that I'd seen before.

Next month it was at twenty-eight. Next quarter, fifteen. I held. I held the way a man holds a steering wheel as the car goes over the cliff.

Next, behind the paywall: the day I finally sold, and the phone call to my brother-in-law.

issues.sentence: Brent said, "Hey Lou, what can I do for you today."
issues.issue: Missing question mark - this is clearly a question
issues.fix: Brent said, "Hey Lou, what can I do for you today?"

issues.sentence: At fifty-four I had been almost a millionaire on paper. At forty-five I would have had a great down payment on a house in a market that was about to triple over the next decade. At nine I had, after taxes and after paying off the credit card and after paying back my brother-in-law and after the IRA penalty, enough money to buy a used Subaru Outback.
issues.issue: The third sentence is too long and convoluted with too many prepositional phrases
issues.fix: At nine dollars, after taxes and penalties, after paying off debts and my brother-in-law, I had enough money to buy a used Subaru Outback.

issues.sentence: I did the math the way I had done it in March 2000, on the back of a receipt from the payphone area, except this time the math went in the other direction.
issues.issue: Awkward phrasing - 'from the payphone area' feels clunky
issues.fix: I did the math the way I had done it in March 2000, on the back of a receipt, except this time the math went in the other direction.

issues.problem: Timeline inconsistency with stock price math. The narrator mentions being 'almost a millionaire on paper' at $54/share and having 'a great down payment' at $45/share, but then says this happened 'in March 2000' when doing the math 'on the back of a receipt from the payphone area.' This creates confusion about when the math was done and whether these higher prices occurred before or after March 2000.
issues.fix: Clarify the timeline by specifying when the stock reached $54 and $45, and make it clear that the March 2000 math was done at a different price point (likely much higher, given the dot-com bubble peak).

issues.problem: The phrase 'I did the math the way I had done it in March 2000, on the back of a receipt from the payphone area' is confusing because it suggests the narrator was doing math at the same payphone location in March 2000, but the context implies this is about recalculating losses from different price points.
issues.fix: Rewrite to clarify that this is about recalculating the same type of math (potential gains/losses) but in reverse, rather than suggesting the same physical location.

issues.problem: Minor inconsistency: The narrator mentions putting money 'in a Vanguard fund and tried not to look at it for a year' but then immediately transitions to telling his wife, creating a temporal disconnect in the narrative flow.
issues.fix: Either clarify this is what he did later with the remaining money, or restructure the sequence to maintain chronological order.

coherence.rewritten_body:
I sold at nine dollars a share on a Thursday in April 2001 from a payphone outside a Quiznos.

I'd like to tell you I sold from a Bloomberg terminal in a corner office with a view of a river. I sold from a payphone outside a Quiznos in a strip mall because my home internet was out, my work computer was being reimaged by IT, and my flip phone could not handle the brokerage website. I had driven to the Quiznos because there was a Kinko's two doors down where I could use a computer for nine dollars an hour, but the Kinko's computers were all taken by a group of people who appeared to be filing a class action lawsuit against something.

So: the payphone. I called my broker. His name was, let's say, Brent. Brent had a voice like a guy who had recently been promoted from cold-calling and was still excited about it.

Brent said, "Hey Lou, what can I do for you today."

I told him to sell all of it. Every share. Market order.

Brent paused. Brent said, "Lou, you sure? The stock's at, uh, $9.14 right now."

I said I was sure.

Brent said, "It's down a lot from where you, uh, where you might have wanted to —"

I told Brent I was aware of where I might have wanted to. I told Brent to sell.

Brent sold.

There's a moment, when you finally pull the trigger on a position that has been killing you for fourteen months, where you expect to feel relief. I had been promised this feeling by a personal finance book I'd been reading in secret, in the bathroom, like a teenager with a Playboy. The book said: the moment you accept the loss and act, you feel free.

I did not feel free. I felt like I had vomited into my own life and was now standing next to it.

I hung up the payphone. The receiver had a sticker on it that said FOR A GOOD TIME CALL with a number that had been crossed out and replaced with another number that had been crossed out. I stood there in the parking lot of the Quiznos for what felt like a long time but was probably four minutes. There was a woman loading groceries into a Dodge Caravan and her toddler was screaming. The toddler was screaming because the woman would not let the toddler hold a watermelon. The watermelon was bigger than the toddler. The toddler did not care. The toddler wanted the watermelon and could not have it and the toddler was expressing this in the only way available.

I understood the toddler.

I got in my Civic — the one with the perfectly functional transmission — and I sat there with the keys in the ignition and I did the math. I did the math the same way I had been doing it for months, calculating what different exit prices would mean, except this time the math was final.

At fifty-four dollars a share, back in early 2000, I had been almost a millionaire on paper. At forty-five I would have had enough for a great down payment on a house in a market that was about to triple over the next decade. At nine I had, after taxes and after paying off the credit card and after paying back my brother-in-law and after the IRA penalty, enough money to buy a used Subaru Outback.

Then I drove home and I had to tell my wife.

She was in the kitchen making spaghetti. The pineapple magnet was no longer on the fridge. It had migrated to a drawer about six months earlier, the way these things do, without ceremony. The fridge now had a magnet from the dry cleaner.

I told her I'd sold. I told her the number.

She stirred the spaghetti. She did not look at me. She said, "Okay."

She said it the way you say "okay" when there is nothing else to say and you have been waiting a long time to not say it.

The spaghetti was overcooked. We ate it anyway. The toddler in the parking lot, somewhere across town, was probably still screaming about the watermelon.

I did not buy a used Subaru Outback. I put what was left in a Vanguard fund and tried not to look at it for a year.

Next: the phone call to my brother-in-law. The lie. The transmission that never needed fixing.

issues.sentence: My brother-in-law's name is — let's call him Rick — and I owed him four thousand dollars and a story.
issues.issue: Repetitive - exact same sentence as hook, creates redundancy
issues.fix: Remove this sentence entirely since it repeats the hook

issues.sentence: Not in 2000, when I should have. Not in 2001, when I sold at nine and the not-telling became its own ongoing crime. Not in 2003, at his daughter's first birthday, when he made a joke about how I'd been driving the same Civic for ten years and clearly his loan had bought me eternal automotive life.
issues.issue: Repetitive sentence structure - three consecutive sentences starting with 'Not in/at'
issues.fix: Vary the structure: 'I should have told him in 2000. In 2001, when I sold at nine, the silence became its own crime. By 2003, at his daughter's first birthday, he was joking...'

issues.sentence: Not at any of the four Thanksgivings between 2000 and 2004, when he kept the joke alive like a cactus, watering it once a year with just enough laughter to keep it from dying.
issues.issue: Metaphor feels forced and awkward - cacti don't need much water, so 'watering it once a year' doesn't work logically
issues.fix: Remove the metaphor: 'Not during any of the four Thanksgivings between 2000 and 2004, when he kept the joke alive with just enough annual laughter to sustain it.'

issues.sentence: A transmission was a problem a man could have. A Roth IRA penalty plus a credit card plus a stock buy was a problem a different kind of man had, and I wasn't ready to be that man in front of Rick.
issues.issue: Repetitive structure with 'A [thing] was a problem a [type of] man [could] have'
issues.fix: Combine for flow: 'A transmission was a respectable problem. A Roth IRA penalty, maxed credit cards, and gambling on stocks - that was a different kind of problem entirely, and I wasn't ready to be that man in front of Rick.'

issues.sentence: Rick chewed. Rick swallowed. Rick said, 'Huh.'
issues.issue: Overly repetitive sentence structure - three short sentences starting with 'Rick'
issues.fix: Vary the rhythm: 'Rick chewed, swallowed. 'Huh,' he said.'

issues.sentence: Rick whistled. Rick said, 'Lou, you absolute donkey.'
issues.issue: Repetitive structure following the previous Rick sentences
issues.fix: Combine: 'Rick whistled low. 'Lou, you absolute donkey.'

readability.rewritten_body:
The four thousand I had paid back in March 2000, two weeks after the IPO popped, with a check that had a memo line reading "TRANSMISSION — THANKS BRO." Rick had laughed when he got it. He had called me and said, "Civic running smooth?" and I had said, "Like a dream," and we had moved on.

The story was harder.

I should have told Rick the truth in 2000. In 2001, when I sold at nine, the silence became its own ongoing crime. By 2003, at his daughter's first birthday, he was joking about how I'd been driving the same Civic for ten years and clearly his loan had bought me eternal automotive life. During the four Thanksgivings between 2000 and 2004, he kept the joke alive with just enough annual laughter to sustain it.

The cowardice was layered. I want you to see the layers.

Layer one: I did not tell him at the start because I was embarrassed to ask for money to gamble with. The transmission lie was easier. A transmission was a respectable problem. A Roth IRA penalty, maxed credit cards, and gambling on stocks — that was a different kind of problem entirely, and I wasn't ready to be that man in front of Rick.

Layer two: I did not tell him after the IPO popped because telling him then would have meant telling him I'd made a lot of money on something he'd unknowingly funded. He would have been happy for me. He would also, somewhere in his head, have run the math on what his share might have been if I'd cut him in. I didn't want him to run that math. So I kept quiet.

Layer three: I did not tell him after I sold at nine because by then the lie had aged into a fixture. Telling him in 2001 would have meant explaining not just the lie but the year of additional lying. And the loss. And the fact that I'd lost not just my own money but, in a sense, his goodwill, which he didn't even know he'd given.

Layer four, and this is the worst one: I started avoiding him. Not dramatically. Just enough. I skipped two cookouts in 2002 with vague excuses. I let his calls go to voicemail and returned them at hours I knew he wouldn't pick up. My wife noticed. She didn't push.

Layer five: when I finally told him, in 2021, at a Thanksgiving with two glasses of wine in me and four in him, I told him in the worst possible way. I told him while passing the green beans. I said, "Rick, that loan in '99 wasn't for the transmission." He looked at me. I said, "It was for a stock buy. The company IPO'd. I made some money. I lost most of it. I paid you back fine, but I lied about what it was for, and I'm sorry."

There was a silence at the table that had a shape. My niece, now grown, looked at her phone. My mother-in-law looked at her plate. My wife looked at me with an expression I'd seen exactly once before, the night I sold at nine, and I understood then that she had known. Of course she had known. She had known for twenty-one years and had let me carry it alone because she had been waiting for me to put it down myself.

Rick chewed, swallowed. "Huh," he said.

Then: "So what'd you make?"

I told him the number at the peak. I told him the number when I sold.

Rick whistled low. "Lou, you absolute donkey."

And that should have been the end of it. It was not the end of it. The transmission joke, which had been a low hum for two decades, became a feature. Rick now mentions it at every family gathering. He introduces me to strangers as "the guy who lied about a transmission." He brings it up in front of waiters. He brought it up at my niece's college graduation last spring. He will bring it up this weekend at a lake house, and I am — and this is the part I'm not proud of — planning what I'm going to say back.

issues.problem: Timeline inconsistency: The narrator says he borrowed money in '99, IPO popped in March 2000, but then says Rick made a joke about driving the same Civic for 'ten years' at his daughter's first birthday in 2003. If the loan was in 1999, that would only be 4 years, not 10.
issues.fix: Either change the loan date to 1993 to match the 'ten years' reference, or change the joke to reference a shorter timeframe like 'four years' or 'since college'

issues.problem: Character action inconsistency: The narrator claims he was avoiding Rick starting in 2002, but then says he attended 'four Thanksgivings between 2000 and 2004' where Rick kept the transmission joke alive. If he was avoiding Rick, how was he present for all these family gatherings?
issues.fix: Either remove the claim about avoiding Rick, or specify that he attended some but not all family gatherings, or clarify that the avoidance was selective (cookouts but not major holidays)

issues.problem: Logical gap: The narrator says his wife 'had known for twenty-one years' when he confessed in 2021, but the original loan was in 1999 (22 years prior). The math doesn't add up.
issues.fix: Change to 'twenty-two years' or adjust the confession year to 2020

coherence.rewritten_body:
My brother-in-law's name is — let's call him Rick — and I owed him four thousand dollars and a story.

The four thousand I had paid back in March 2000, two weeks after the IPO popped, with a check that had a memo line reading "TRANSMISSION — THANKS BRO." Rick had laughed when he got it. He had called me and said, "Civic running smooth?" and I had said, "Like a dream," and we had moved on.

The story was harder.

I did not tell Rick the truth for a long time. Not in 2000, when I should have. Not in 2001, when I sold at nine and the not-telling became its own ongoing crime. Not in 2003, at his daughter's first birthday, when he made a joke about how I'd been driving the same Civic for four years and clearly his loan had bought me eternal automotive life. Not at most of the Thanksgivings between 2000 and 2004, when he kept the joke alive like a cactus, watering it once a year with just enough laughter to keep it from dying.

The cowardice was layered. I want you to see the layers.

Layer one: I did not tell him at the start because I was embarrassed to ask for money to gamble with. The transmission lie was easier. A transmission was a problem a man could have. A Roth IRA penalty plus a credit card plus a stock buy was a problem a different kind of man had, and I wasn't ready to be that man in front of Rick.

Layer two: I did not tell him after the IPO popped because telling him then would have meant telling him I'd made a lot of money on something he'd unknowingly funded. He would have been happy for me. He would also, somewhere in his head, have run the math on what his share might have been if I'd cut him in. I didn't want him to run that math. So I kept quiet.

Layer three: I did not tell him after I sold at nine because by then the lie had aged into a fixture. Telling him in 2001 would have meant explaining not just the lie but the year of additional lying. And the loss. And the fact that I'd lost not just my own money but, in a sense, his goodwill, which he didn't even know he'd given.

Layer four, and this is the worst one: I started pulling back from him. Not dramatically. Just enough. I skipped two cookouts in 2002 with vague excuses. I let some of his calls go to voicemail and returned them at hours I knew he wouldn't pick up. At family holidays, I was present but distant. My wife noticed. She didn't push.

Layer five: when I finally told him, in 2021, at a Thanksgiving with two glasses of wine in me and four in him, I told him in the worst possible way. I told him while passing the green beans. I said, "Rick, that loan in '99 wasn't for the transmission." He looked at me. I said, "It was for a stock buy. The company IPO'd. I made some money. I lost most of it. I paid you back fine, but I lied about what it was for, and I'm sorry."

There was a silence at the table that had a shape. My niece, now grown, looked at her phone. My mother-in-law looked at her plate. My wife looked at me with an expression I'd seen exactly once before, the night I sold at nine, and I understood then that she had known. Of course she had known. She had known for twenty-two years and had let me carry it alone because she had been waiting for me to put it down myself.

Rick chewed. Rick swallowed. Rick said, "Huh."

Then Rick said, "So what'd you make?"

I told him the number at the peak. I told him the number when I sold.

Rick whistled. Rick said, "Lou, you absolute donkey."

And that should have been the end of it. It was not the end of it. The transmission joke, which had been a low hum for two decades, became a feature. Rick now mentions it at every family gathering. He introduces me to strangers as "the guy who lied about a transmission." He brings it up in front of waiters. He brought it up at my niece's college graduation last spring. He will bring it up this weekend at a lake house, and I am — and this is the part I'm not proud of — planning what I'm going to say back.

Next: the rules. The ones I follow now. The ones I should have followed then.

issues.sentence: These are the rules I follow now because the version of me who didn't follow them is still paying for the Quiznos payphone and the navy tie and the pineapple magnet.
issues.issue: Unclear references - readers don't know what these specific items mean yet, making the sentence confusing
issues.fix: These are the rules I follow now because the version of me who didn't follow them is still paying the price.

issues.sentence: The decision has to be made when the price isn't moving on a screen in front of you, because the screen is a casino and your brain on the screen is a tourist with a free drink coupon.
issues.issue: Awkward phrasing with 'your brain on the screen' - unclear and doesn't flow well
issues.fix: The decision has to be made when the price isn't moving on a screen in front of you, because the screen is a casino and you become a tourist with a free drink coupon.

issues.sentence: it just made her carry the truth and the hiding of the truth at the same time.
issues.issue: Repetitive use of 'the truth' makes the sentence clunky
issues.fix: it just made her carry both the reality and the burden of my deception.

issues.sentence: It was a different company in a different market in a different country called The Future, and I was holding shares of a country called The Past.
issues.issue: Overuse of 'different' and the metaphor becomes confusing with countries
issues.fix: It was a new company in a new market operating in The Future, while I was holding shares of The Past.

issues.problem: Timeline inconsistency: The author refers to himself as '31-year-old Lou' but then mentions 'twenty-four years later' in the tease. If he's writing this 24 years after the events when he was 31, he would now be 55, but this isn't clearly established.
issues.fix: Clarify the current timeframe or adjust the numbers to be consistent

issues.problem: Character introduction inconsistency: 'Dan from finance' is introduced without context, then later referred to as a colleague who gave advice in a kitchenette. The narrative flow makes it unclear who Dan is initially.
issues.fix: Either introduce Dan properly the first time he's mentioned, or restructure to introduce him in the kitchenette section first

issues.problem: Unclear reference: 'Rick' is mentioned as someone who would have been asked for a $4,000 loan, but it's unclear who Rick is (family member, friend, etc.) until the phrase 'loans from family' appears.
issues.fix: Clarify Rick's relationship to the author earlier in that paragraph

issues.problem: Vague opening references: The 'Quiznos payphone and the navy tie and the pineapple magnet' are mentioned as if they're significant, but they're never explained or referenced again in the body.
issues.fix: Either explain these references or remove them, as they create expectation without payoff

issues.problem: Stock price progression unclear: The narrative mentions selling at various prices (fifty-four, twenty-eight, nine) but doesn't clearly establish this as a chronological decline or explain the context.
issues.fix: Clarify that these represent the stock's decline over time and provide better context for when each price point occurred

issues.sentence: I drive past the house I would have bought about once a month.
issues.issue: This sentence is repeated verbatim from the hook, creating unnecessary redundancy
issues.fix: Remove this repetitive opening sentence and start directly with 'I don't do it on purpose.'

issues.sentence: It was listed at $340,000. Today it'd go for north of a million and a half, easy.
issues.issue: Two very short sentences create choppy rhythm
issues.fix: It was listed at $340,000, but today it'd go for north of a million and a half, easy.

issues.sentence: I have a wife — a different one, a second one, a better one for me at this stage — who knows the whole story and doesn't make jokes about it.
issues.issue: The nested dashes create a convoluted structure that's hard to follow when read aloud
issues.fix: I have a second wife, one who's better for me at this stage, who knows the whole story and doesn't make jokes about it.

issues.sentence: I have a daughter who calls. I have a hardware store guy named Mel. I have a Civic — a different Civic — that genuinely did need a transmission in 2019 and I paid for it in cash and I thought about Rick when I signed the receipt.
issues.issue: The repetitive 'I have' structure followed by one very long sentence creates jarring contrast
issues.fix: I have a daughter who calls and a hardware store guy named Mel. When my Civic needed a transmission in 2019, I paid for it in cash and thought about Rick when I signed the receipt.

issues.problem: Timeline inconsistency: The narrator says he was 'running the numbers' in March 2000 but later states his daughter is 'now older than I was when all this happened.' If she's older than he was in 2000, she'd be at least in her mid-40s, but she's asking basic questions about Roth IRAs, which suggests she's much younger - likely in her 20s or early 30s.
issues.fix: Either change the year to make the timeline work (perhaps 2010-2015 range), or clarify that the daughter is asking about Roth IRAs despite being older, or adjust the daughter's age reference.

issues.problem: The narrative implies a single catastrophic financial decision/mistake, but the specific nature of this decision is never clearly established. References to 'Rick,' 'transmission,' trading windows, and share prices suggest different types of financial situations that don't clearly connect.
issues.fix: Clarify the central financial mistake - was it a failed stock trade, a bad investment with Rick, or something else? The various references should point to one coherent financial disaster.

issues.problem: The narrator mentions having 'a different disaster' in alternate timelines like 'a sicker kid' but earlier mentions having only one child instead of the two he would have had in the house. This creates confusion about his actual family situation.
issues.fix: Clarify whether he has one child or multiple, and ensure the alternate timeline references align with his actual family structure.

issues.problem: Post 3 ends with lockup day drama but Post 4 jumps to selling in April 2001 - we never see what happened on lockup day itself
issues.fix: Either show the lockup day outcome in Post 3, or have Post 4 acknowledge/reference what happened on lockup day before jumping to the April sale

issues.problem: Post 6 promises to show 'what this still costs me' but Post 7's house story feels more wistful than costly - the emotional weight doesn't match the setup
issues.fix: Either make Post 7 show a more concrete ongoing cost, or adjust Post 6's tease to match the reflective tone of the house story

consistency_issues.contradiction: Post 5 says he paid Rick back 'in March 2000, two weeks after the IPO popped' but earlier posts establish he was holding through 2001 - why would he pay back the loan before selling?
Post 3 ends on lockup day with stock at $43, building tension, but Post 4 jumps to selling at $9 in April 2001 without explaining the dramatic drop or what happened on lockup day
